---
project:
  type: website
  output-dir: docs
  render:
    - index.qmd
    - about.qmd
    - report.qmd
    - interactive-viz.qmd

website:
  title: "Predicting S&P 500 Returns with Macro-Financial Indicators"
  navbar:
    left:
      - text: "Home"
        href: index.qmd
      - text: "Final Report"
        href: report.qmd
      - text: "Interactive Visualizations"
        href: interactive-viz.qmd
      - text: "About"
        href: about.qmd
    right:
      - icon: github
        href: https://github.com/lijack032/JSC370-Project
        target: _blank

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: right
    code-overflow: scroll
    css: styles.css

execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
  cache: true

jupyter: python3
---

---
title: "Predicting S&P 500 Returns with Macro-Financial Indicators"
---

# Project Overview

Financial markets respond to a wide range of economic signals, including interest rates, inflation, labor market conditions, investor sentiment, commodity prices, and volatility. This project examines whether these publicly available **macro-financial indicators** contain useful information for predicting future **S&P 500 returns**.

The central research question is:

**Can macro-financial indicators meaningfully predict future S&P 500 returns, and do machine learning methods improve predictive accuracy relative to simpler benchmark and linear models?**

To answer this question, the project combines daily financial market data from **Yahoo Finance** with macroeconomic indicators from **Federal Reserve Economic Data (FRED)**. The analysis evaluates two related prediction tasks:

1. **Regression:** predicting the numerical size of future S&P 500 returns  
2. **Classification:** predicting whether future S&P 500 returns are positive or non-positive  

Both tasks are evaluated across **1-day**, **5-day**, and **20-day** forecast horizons.

## Data and Variables

The dataset combines market-based indicators with broader macroeconomic measures. These variables were chosen to capture different channels through which financial and economic conditions may affect equity returns.

Key variables include:

- **Equity market indicators:** S&P 500 Index and Dow Jones Industrial Average
- **Interest-rate indicators:** 10-year Treasury yield, 1-year Treasury yield, and Federal Funds Rate
- **Risk and uncertainty indicators:** CBOE Volatility Index and credit spread
- **Macroeconomic indicators:** Consumer Price Index, unemployment rate, industrial production, and consumer sentiment
- **Market condition indicators:** U.S. Dollar Index and crude oil prices

The final modeling dataset also includes engineered predictors such as lagged variables, rolling averages, rolling volatility, momentum measures, short-run changes, and yield-spread features.

## Methodological Approach

The project uses a chronological **training / validation / test split** to reflect the structure of a real forecasting problem. The models are trained on earlier observations, tuned using a later validation period, and evaluated on a final held-out test period. This avoids random shuffling and reduces look-ahead bias.

The model comparison includes:

- **Benchmark models:** historical mean forecast and majority-class classifier
- **Linear models:** Ordinary Least Squares and Logistic Regression
- **Regularized models:** Ridge Regression and Lasso Regression
- **Machine learning models:** Random Forest and XGBoost

Hyperparameters were tuned using **Optuna**. Regression models were evaluated using **RMSE**, **MAE**, and out-of-sample **$R^2$**. Classification models were evaluated using **accuracy** and **ROC-AUC**.

## Main Findings

The results suggest that predicting the exact size of future S&P 500 returns is highly difficult. Most regression models produced out-of-sample $R^2$ values close to zero or negative. The strongest regression result came from the **1-day Random Forest model**, but its improvement over the benchmark was very small, suggesting limited practical predictive power.

Classification results were somewhat more promising, but still modest. **Logistic Regression** performed best across all three forecast horizons based on ROC-AUC, with the strongest result at the **20-day horizon**. This suggests that macro-financial indicators may contain some information about medium-term market direction, even though the signal is not strong enough to support highly accurate forecasting.

The model interpretation results also show that different prediction tasks rely on different types of information. Short-term return prediction was driven mainly by volatility and momentum variables, while the 20-day direction model placed more weight on broader macro-financial indicators such as interest rates, unemployment, inflation, oil prices, and credit spreads.

Overall, the findings support a mixed conclusion: macro-financial indicators provide little evidence of meaningful predictability for exact return size, but they show limited usefulness for classifying medium-term market direction.

## Project Materials

- [Read the full final report](report.html)
- [View interactive visualizations](interactive-viz.html)
- [Download the PDF report](report.pdf)
- [View the GitHub repository](https://github.com/lijack032/JSC370-Project)
